title: Water softeners versus salt-free conditioners
subtitle: What each system does, where it's worth fitting, and the water and maintenance costs
excerpt: Ion-exchange water softeners remove hardness minerals entirely and require salt and a wastewater discharge. Salt-free conditioners change the crystal form of calcium without removing it: no salt, no drain, and no softened feel. The right choice depends on hardness level, budget, and whether soft water is actually the goal. This covers the mechanics, running costs, and when each is justified.
sourceNotes: Water Treatment Association UK guidance. WRAS (Water Regulations Advisory Scheme) installation requirements. Which? water softener review methodology and cost data.

glossaryTerms.definition: A measure of dissolved calcium and magnesium carbonate in the water supply. Soft water is under 60 mg/l; moderately hard 60 to 120 mg/l; hard 120 to 180 mg/l; very hard above 180 mg/l. Most of southern England is above 200 mg/l.

content.text: In hard water areas (broadly: London, the South East, the Midlands, and East Anglia), limescale deposits on heating elements and in pipes reduce boiler and appliance efficiency. At 200 mg/l

, a kettle element builds a 1 mm scale layer in approximately 3 months of daily use, which reduces heating efficiency by 7 to 10%. A boiler heat exchanger scaled to 6 mm uses 15 to 25% more energy. Whether a water treatment system is cost-effective depends on the starting hardness level and what appliances it protects.

Ion-exchange softeners pass the mains water through a resin bed that swaps calcium and magnesium ions for sodium ions. The output is fully soft water: no scale, no need to squeegee shower screens, different feel on skin and hair. The resin must be regenerated periodically with brine (saltwater), using 30 to 60 litres per regeneration cycle and discharging concentrated brine to the drain. Running costs: 10 to 20 kg of salt per month and 500 to 1,000 litres of regeneration water per month. The outgoing soft water is high in sodium and should not be used for infant formula or drinking by those on low-sodium diets.

Salt-free conditioners use TAC (template-assisted crystallisation) or electromagnetic processes to change the way calcium deposits form, without removing it from the water. The hardness level stays the same, but calcium forms a crystal type that does not stick to heating elements. TAC systems perform well in tests. Magnetic and electronic clip-on units are less reliable. A certified TAC conditioner on the cold supply protects a boiler heat exchanger with very low maintenance. Replace the TAC media every 3 to 5 years; running cost is otherwise near-zero.

Above 300 mg/l hardness (very hard): ion-exchange softener is likely cost-effective if protecting a combi boiler, radiators, and appliances. Payback period 3 to 5 years at current salt prices versus appliance descaling and replacement costs.
150 to 300 mg/l hardness (hard): either system offers useful protection. A TAC conditioner is lower commitment and avoids the salt cost and drain requirement.
Below 150 mg/l (moderate): scale builds slowly. Regular appliance descaling every 3 to 6 months is probably sufficient with no installed system.
